body:
**TL;DR:** Browser-mode (`--capture browser`) Google Docs extraction has two bugs that affect both the Rust and JS CLIs identically. Both stem from heuristics in the `DOCS_modelChunk` parser that were over-fitted to the project's existing public test fixture and fail on real documents.

## Versions affected

- `web-capture` (Rust crate): **0.3.9** (latest published)
- `@link-assistant/web-capture` (npm): **1.7.17** (latest published)

Both fully reproduce the bugs below.

## Public reproducer doc

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Rvaod_u2wgkAUNdXG-e29yQem-P36KAQ/edit (Anyone with the link, viewer)

This is a copy of the existing markdown-feature test fixture with a new **Section 15** added that contains three small structures designed to trigger the bugs. The original sections (1‚Äì14) still pass ‚Äî the regressions only show in the new Section 15.

## Bug 1 ‚Äî Numbered list rendered as `<ul>` (markdown `- `)

### Reproduction

In Section 15, subsection A of the public reproducer:

```
1. Apple
2. Banana
3. Cherry
```

This is an ordered list (decimal markers) in the source. Confirm via `--capture api`, which preserves it correctly:

`--capture api` markdown:
```
1. Apple
2. Banana
3. Cherry
```

`--capture browser` markdown (BUG):
```
- Apple
- Banana
- Cherry
```

### Root cause (Rust)

`rust/src/gdocs.rs:2473`:

```rust
fn infer_ordered_list(list: &ListMeta, text: &str) -> bool {
    let ordered_id = matches!(
        list.id.as_str(),
        "kix.list.7" | "kix.list.8" | "kix.list.9" | "kix.list.10" | "kix.list.11" | "kix.list.13"
    );
    ordered_id
        && (text.contains("ordered")
            || text.contains("Parent item")
            || text.contains("Child item")
            || text.contains("Grandchild item")
            || text.contains("First item")
            || text.contains("Second item")
            || text.contains("Third item")
            || text.contains("Ordered child"))
}
```

This is a hardcoded list-ID allowlist combined with a content-substring check. It returns `false` for any document whose lists don't have one of those specific IDs and whose items don't contain those specific substrings ‚Äî i.e. for essentially every real document. The only doc it works on is the existing public test fixture, because the heuristic was fitted to that fixture's content.

### Root cause (JS)

`js/src/gdocs.js` ~L946:

```js
function inferOrderedList(list, text) {
  if (
    /ordered|^\d+(?:\.|\))|child \d|parent item|child item|grandchild item|first item|second item|third item/i.test(text)
  ) {
    return /^kix\.list\.(7|8|9|10|11|13)$/u.test(list.id);
  }
  return false;
}
```

Identical defective approach.

### Why a heuristic at all?

I confirmed by capturing live `DOCS_modelChunk` data (using a `Object.defineProperty` hook installed via `addInitScript` before page load) that the chunk does NOT carry glyph-type / ordered-vs-unordered information for the list. The schema has:

- `ty="as"` mutations with `st="list"` carry only `ls_id` (list ID), `ls_ts` (text-styling for the marker), and `ls_c` (with sub-fields `c_cs`/`c_tdai` ‚Äî neither encodes glyph type).
- A full keyword search across the chunk for `ordered|decimal|numbered|bullet|disc|circle|square|lower-latin|lower-roman` returns zero matches.

So the parser cannot answer "is this list ordered?" from `DOCS_modelChunk` alone.

The api-mode HTML export DOES have this information ‚Äî Google embeds it in the `<style>` block as CSS counter rules:

```css
.lst-kix_8pt4o74780u8-0>li:before{content:"" counter(lst-ctn-kix_8pt4o74780u8-0,decimal) ". "}
```

The `decimal` argument (vs absent / `disc` / `none`) tells you the glyph is numeric.

### Suggested fix

Two reasonable paths:

1. **Always fetch api-mode HTML alongside browser-mode** (a tiny, fast public export request) and merge: walk the api-mode CSS to build `Map<list_id, glyph_type>`, then use that to decide `ordered` for each list seen in the chunk. This requires the doc to be public-export-capable; for private docs the user is already authenticated to do `--capture browser`, so an `/export?format=html` request with the same cookies should also succeed.

2. **Default to `<ol>` when `ps_il > 0` AND `ps_ifl < ps_il` AND the marker text from `ls_ts` is a digit/letter sequence**. This is also a heuristic but a much more principled one (the per-paragraph `ps_il`/`ps_ifl` fields are stable and the `ls_ts` text-styling is per-list).

Either way, drop the hardcoded list-ID allowlist and content-substring regex.

## Bug 2 ‚Äî Continuation paragraphs misclassified as `<blockquote>`

### Reproduction

In Section 15, subsection B of the public reproducer:

> 1. Step one
>
>    Continuation paragraph that is not a list item. Same indent as Step one.
>
> 1. Step two

(Created in the editor by: type "1. Step one", Enter, Backspace to drop the marker but keep the indent, type the continuation paragraph, Enter, re-apply numbered list, "Step two".)

`--capture api` markdown: keeps the continuation as a regular paragraph (no blockquote prefix).

`--capture browser` markdown (BUG):
```
- Step one

> Continuation paragraph that is not a list item. Same indent as Step one.

- Step two
```

The non-list paragraph between Step one and Step two is emitted as a blockquote.

### Root cause (Rust)

`rust/src/gdocs.rs:2458`:

```rust
} else if paragraph_style.is_some_and(|style| {
    style.indent_start > 0.0
        && (style.indent_start - style.indent_first_line).abs() < f64::EPSILON
}) {
    meta.quote = true;
}
```

The heuristic is "any non-list paragraph with `indent_start > 0` and `indent_start == indent_first_line`" ‚Üí blockquote. But that's exactly the shape of a Google Docs list-continuation paragraph (when you Backspace out the marker, the indent is preserved equally on first-line and subsequent lines).

### Suggested fix

Either:
1. Cross-check with api-mode HTML: api correctly emits `<blockquote>` only when the source has one. Use it as the source of truth for which indented paragraphs are quotes.
2. Tighten the heuristic: require either (a) a known blockquote paragraph style (`HEADING_*`-equivalent style id for blockquote), or (b) the indent to be substantially larger than typical list-item indent (e.g. `> 36pt`, the typical level-0 list indent), or (c) the paragraph not to be sandwiched between list items.

## Why both bugs were missed by CI

`gdocs-public-doc.test.js` and `gdocs_public_doc.rs` test against the existing public fixture (`1f5zI2xOFpKa90v0GjamO_t7lqSdzMlaM`). That fixture's content matches the heuristic regexes ("First item", "Second item", "Parent item", etc.) and its list IDs are within the hardcoded allowlist. So the tests pass for reasons that don't generalize.

Suggested test additions: capture the public reproducer doc above (or a similar synthetic one) and assert both:
- numbered lists with content like "Apple", "Banana" produce `1. ‚Ä¶ 2. ‚Ä¶` markdown, not `- ‚Ä¶ - ‚Ä¶`
- list-item continuation paragraphs do NOT become blockquotes

## Side note (not blocking)

In the same Section 15 of the reproducer, browser-mode also wraps the entire section's text in italics (`*Apple*`, `*Banana*`, `*Step one*`, etc.) ‚Äî likely italic-style leakage from the preceding italic "End of Markdown Feature Test Document" line. API-mode renders the same text upright. This may be a separate text-style range tracking bug; mentioning here for visibility but happy to file separately if preferred.

## Tested with

- macOS 14 (Darwin 24.6.0)
- Chromium fetched by `chromiumoxide` (Rust) / Playwright (JS)
- Both CLIs installed via their published distributions (`npm i -g`, `cargo install`)
title: --capture browser: numbered lists rendered as <ul> + non-list paragraphs misclassified as <blockquote>
